reasoning: The CDC's 2019 Antimicrobial Resistance Threats Report estimated 35,900 AMR deaths in the US, noting an 18% decrease from their retroactively adjusted 2013 baseline (which was revised to around 44,000). A 2022 special CDC report found that hospital-onset AMR deaths increased by about 15% in 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic. A 15% increase off a ~35,900 baseline puts the figure at roughly 41,000—still very far from 70,000. Post-pandemic, US hospitalizations fell and AMR stewardship likely rebounded, meaning the death trend likely flatlined or resumed its downward trajectory. Furthermore, the updated Global Research on Antimicrobial Resistance (GRAM 2.0) study published in September 2024 found that global AMR deaths slightly decreased from 1.2 million in 2019 to 1.14 million in 2021, projecting slow growth by 2050. This confirms that there has been no dramatic doubling in the burden either globally or in high-income countries like the US. While there are some older academic papers (e.g., Burnham et al., 2018) estimating upwards of 150,000 deaths using looser ICD-code based methodologies, they were not widely adopted by public health authorities as the institutional standard; if they had been considered a sufficient standard for 'another credible source', the question would have already resolved. With the question's year-end deadline rapidly approaching and no new report nearing the 70,000 figure, it is highly unlikely to resolve YES.
